fundamental_rights: Article 20 — Protection against arbitrary arrest (no ex post facto laws, no double jeopardy)
Article 21 — Right to Life and Personal Liberty (includes right to fair trial and legal aid)
Article 22 — Protection against arbitrary arrest (right to know grounds, right to lawyer, 24-hour magistrate rule)
Article 39A — Right to free legal aid for accused who cannot afford a lawyer

laws.name: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023 (replaces CrPC)
sections: Section 35 (arrest without warrant by police — grounds required)
Section 47 (right of arrested person to inform family)
Section 52 (examination of arrested person by doctor)
laws.summary: Governs criminal procedure from FIR to trial. Key right: must be produced before Magistrate within 24 hours. You have a right to a lawyer.
laws.remedy: File petition in Sessions Court for bail; approach High Court for writ if rights violated

laws.name: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 (replaces IPC)
sections: Section 103 (murder — life imprisonment or death penalty)
laws.summary: New criminal code replacing IPC. Defines crimes and punishments.
laws.remedy: File FIR at police station; police must register a cognisable offence

laws.name: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA), 2023 (replaces Indian Evidence Act)
laws.summary: Governs what evidence is admissible in court. Digital/electronic evidence is now explicitly included.
laws.remedy: Preserve all digital evidence; get seizure memo from police

laws.name: Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) / BNSS
laws.summary: Police MUST register FIR for cognisable offences. If they refuse, approach Superintendent of Police or Magistrate.
laws.remedy: If FIR refused: send complaint to SP by post or approach Magistrate under Section 175 BNSS

steps: If arrested: immediately ask to be informed of the grounds of arrest (your right under Article 22)
Demand your right to inform a family member or friend — police must allow this (BNSS Section 47)
You must be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours — if not, it is illegal detention
Request a lawyer immediately — you have the right to free legal aid (NALSA 15100)
To file an FIR: go to the police station in whose jurisdiction the crime occurred
If police refuse to register FIR: send a written complaint to the Superintendent of Police by registered post
If FIR still not registered: file a complaint before the Magistrate under Section 175 BNSS
For bail: file application in Sessions Court (for serious offences) or before Magistrate (for minor offences)
For anticipatory bail (before arrest): file in Sessions Court or High Court
